tokens: (Unaware that they have hot mikes) I think I know where we are too.
If I can just look at that map.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Be sure I get that all the way up.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Are you ready to do a little urine transfer?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Be advised, you're still on VOX.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I bet we're on hot miked.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Dave and Jim, you're on hot mike now.
(Pause) But the medics are enjoying your comments.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (Pause, listening to Joe) I bet they are.
(Chuckling) I'll bet everybody else is too.
(Hearty laughs from Jim) What did we say, Joe?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We need the circuit breaker in too for that...
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Hey, did you get the comm?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Are we in Down Voice Backup now?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We have a question that may bear on this minor water problem.
We're wondering if you can tell, or have a feel, for whether you're in a crater, or the slope of the spacecraft is, perhaps, caused by just a gentle slope of the lurrain (lunar terrain) there.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I guess, to answer your question, we're not really in a big crater anywhere.
I think, possibly, one gear may be in one of these small craters.
And as you might have heard Jim and I discussing, there's a rather high crater density.
And I guess my references to trafficability were really to boulders, because that's what I was really most concerned with on driving the Rover.
There is a fairly high crater density around.
And, as I mentioned, they range up to probably 8 to 10 meters or so.
And in our local area...Let me give you a rough count of the, oh, 8 to 10 meter ones.
I guess one every 15 to 20 meters.
So there's a fair number of medium craters.
Nothing sharp - no boulders - and it may be that one footpad is in one of these craters that range on down to, maybe, 2 meters or 1 meter.
And then there's a sharp break in craters down to probably a foot or so.
But it's almost like 14 - as I remember their pictures - quite a variety of crater sizes, up to some certain limit.
I don't see anything on the 25-meter scale that we hoped to expose the bedrock in our immediate vicinity.
Although I can see some fresh ones - maybe some rims out through the window here at 10 or 11 o'clock.
But I can't really account for our attitude right now.
We'll just have to get out and take a look.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Copied all that loud and clear.
I phrased my other question poorly.

Apparently, the thermal people were worried that, if you were sitting right in the bowl of a fairly deep crater, there would be a certain focusing effect of the sunlight, and it may require more water to keep the spacecraft cool later on.
That's a good answer we have from you.
We'll have some more questions for you later on when you're comfortable and into your eat period, if you're interested in talking at that time.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: There's so much here, I could talk to you forever.
But there's a large...I can see now...We were in zero-phase and without taking a close look out the front window, I couldn't tell you.
But, as I was coming down trying to select a spot to land, I was trying to avoid these 8- to 10-meter craters.
And we have one out of our, I guess, about 3 or 4 o'clock that I discussed before.
There is one directly in front of us; the rim is almost on the shadow of the radar antenna right now, and it appears to be an 8- to 10-meter one.
And there's one over to our 10 o'clock.
They're just all over, and it was sort of hard to find a spot that was really level.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, Dave, earlier you talked about, specifically, a very bright crater, I think, fairly near by.
Could you estimate for us the size, distance, and azimuth of that bright crater?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (Pause) I can tell you're still looking for our position.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We think we're pretty well squared away on your position.
This probably would cinch it down, though.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: As we're unsuiting here, let me think that one over.
I think we can cinch it down too.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: But, before we go, I got to tell you about a rock that's right out at 12 o'clock, almost at the radar antenna shadow, and it's going to be gone pretty soon.
There's a dark, black, angular fragment which is on the order of, I'd say, 6 to 8 inches across.
It's got some light-colored, apparent dust on it.
All the other fragments appear to be white.
And this one really looks like a jewel.
You can think about that for awhile.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And it wouldn't surprise me at all if there wasn't some thought given to that rock.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And, Dave and Jim, when you're comfortable, we'll call up our best guess as to your position and let you think about that for awhile.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And you'll be pleased to hear that your landing was recorded on either of our seismometers on the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: You can tell the Program Manager (Apollo 9 Commander Jim McDivitt) that we certainly didn't buckle his engine bell for him.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: (Pause) And, Jim, I just have to ask you, did you notice if the contact light came on or not?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Joe, I think Jim might someday qualify what he thought our landing velocity might have been.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I'd say he was dividing everything by 10.
(Pause) And, Dave, be advised the Program...
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Be advised the Program Manager says he'll wait until tomorrow (when Dave and Jim can make a visual inspection) until he decides about that engine bell.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, just tell him that I'll guarantee that it wasn't running when we touched down.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: When you have the ECS configured properly, we'd like to run a (water) separator number 1 check, please.
And we'll ask you to go back to separator number 1.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We would like a mark when you go to separator number 1.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: It'll be a little while, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We'll just know when to be watching.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Hadley for comm check on the lightweights (meaning the lightweight headsets).
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, Jim, standing by for your call.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: If you want to give us the consumables update, we'll take it.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And if you're ready to copy, here they come.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: ...RCS Alfa, 85.0 (percent remaining); Bravo, 85.5; O2 descent number 1, 85; number 2, 83.5; O2 ascent number 1, 99; number 2, 99; H2O descent number 1, 79; number 2, 80; H2O ascent number 1, 100 percent; ascent number 2, 100 percent; amp-hours descent (remaining), 1705; ascent, 572.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Look...That looks pretty close to nominal.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We're in the ECS sleep configuration now, if you want to run your water SEP check.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We're standing by for a mark.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Just for your own information, the water separator number 1 looks good.
But we'll be keeping a further eye on it here.
A little later on, Gordo (CMP CapCom Gordon Fullerton)'s going to read up a procedure that involves (a) VHF communications check.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And, Dave and Jim, we've got some positions, when you get in a comfortable position, maybe have something to eat, but have your maps out.
I'll read them up to you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: If you're not busy, I'll give you some switch positions to get set up for the VHF comm check.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: How much time do we have, Gordo?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Probably about 15 minutes before he comes over the horizon.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Give us about 5 to finish fixing dinner here.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Go ahead with your switch settings.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: You can go ahead and throw these switches as I call them.
We'd like the VHF A Transmitter to Voice, and the VHF A Receiver, On.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And the VHF A Squelch, we'd like you to adjust it.
Suggest (that you adjust it) so you can hear a little noise.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Squelch with a little noise, and Antenna, Aft.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Okay, the LMP, We suggest he makes a check.
Put his Audio panel, VHF A T/R to T/R.
And we're going to have Al initiate the check.
He's kind of busy as he passes over, so he's going to initiate the check at a slack moment, and that'll be sometime between 108:32 and 108:44.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We don't have any mission timer, and we'll be standing by.
And I'll have to do it, because we have one lightweight headset that failed right when we picked it up after lift-off, when we first unstowed it.
We brought it down so Al could have a good one.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We think we know what the problem was.
We think it's just an error in procedures back before PDI and before the comm check.
You were in Voice/Range, according to the Timeline Book, and that would block your transmitter.
We really don't suspect any hardware problem.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We just talked it over, Gordo.
After the radar check, we went from Voice/Range back to Voice on the VHF A.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: But we think that the comm check was before that.
That you got back and that you just never did have another call after you got back to Voice.
The blocking - that was my mistake a minute ago - the block was due to the blocking of your receiver while in ranging.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, we're set up now, and Jim's got his comm helmet on.
So we'll be standing by for Al's call.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Anytime you want to discuss the landing and our position, why, I guess we've got supper cooked, and we're ready.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Understand that supper is cooked or being eaten?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Yeah, if you like cold tomato soup!
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Dave, I guess the first thing that we might start with, is our estimated position of your landing site.
And we've got two inputs on that.
Al, when he passed over, got what seemed like a pretty accurate hack on where you've landed, and he calls it out as Bravo, Romeo 2...Correction.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I tried to find November Crater out there, Joe.
And, I could see a fresh one to the north - a fresh rim - but no bright ejecta, as you see on the map there.
But I guess I probably agree with you.
And I might run through what I saw from pitchover, on down, and that might help you out a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, by the way, your comm is absolutely crystal clear.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: So, anyway, I got the "3000-south" call, which was a good call.
And as we came down prior to P64, I could see the rille to the south, and I couldn't see it up over the nose.
And I got the distinct impression, as I looked at Hadley Delta, coming into P64, that we were going to be way long.
And, I guess...You know, I've never shot one of these landings before, and I got fooled a little bit there.
And, at pitchover, we were definitely quite a ways south.
And I never saw Index Crater all the way down.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I saw what I thought was
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: While you're thinking there, let me repeat a question I asked earlier.
You described a very bright crater in one of your first descriptions.
And we're still looking for the azimuth, approximate distance, and size of that bright crater.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, the brightest crater I've seen is the one that was right on the rim...halfway up St.
And...Is that the one you're thinking about?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I think there was another one.
I'll...I'll get back with you on that in a minute.
It was one that was a lot closer to you.
And I've got another question now on the board in front of me here.
We think you're near the edge of the Aristillus-Aristarchus ray.
And, I wonder if you can recall anything about the local albedo changes.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: No, Joe, I didn't see a thing.
And, it's just all the same (guffaws) north to south, east to west in our current position.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And sorry on that crater call.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (Pause) Dave, while you're sipping your cold tomato soup there, was the black rock that you called out to us on a crater rim?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And it's a difficult crater to see.
It's quite a subtle crater, but it's out - well, the LM shadow being like 30, maybe 28 meters now - it's probably about 40 meters away, the (near) rim of the crater.
And that black rock is sitting right on the rim.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Jim's just pointed out another black one now that must be 300 meters out.
And it's so dark that it looks like a shadow.
It's just coal black, and it looks like it might be about the same size.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: (Pause) While you're peeking out there, do you have any further observations on the abundance, size, and distribution of the frags in the nearby field-of-view?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I'd say that, in the near field, the surface is covered by probably less than 1 percent of fragmental debris (that is, less than one percent of the surface is covered by rock, as opposed to the fine-grain soil).
And, of that debris, I'd say 70 percent of it is on the order of an inch to 2 inches, or less.
And maybe the other 30 percent seems to be in a range of maybe 4 or 5 inches, something like that.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Most the fragments are light colored, except for the two that we mentioned to you.
I can see some that are just stark white and some that are a lighter gray.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: You might comment on the relative abundance.
And, just for your information, (as per checklist) you're coming up on a sleep period in about a half an hour, I guess.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (Probably wondering what happened to the comm) Hey, Houston; 15.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Gee, I'm just looking down right in front of the LM here to try and get your relative abundance, and I was about ready to say that maybe, of these inch frags, there might be five or six in a square meter.
And I see what appears to be a round, glassy ball!
It's shiny; it casts a rounded shadow; and it looks about the size, oh, maybe an inch or so.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I can see some lineaments on the surface which appear to be from the descent engine.
They radiate away from our position here.
We'll take a closer look at those later.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, for the benefit of our Prime Flight Director, maybe the name of that should be called an Aggie.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We'll call that one our first Aggie!
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, Dave, (with regard to the question on the bright crater) you described it as the one near the LM with lighter-gray debris in it.
And I'm sitting here wondering if maybe that was No...
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay; there was one that had fresh debris (that was) light-colored around the rim.
Although it did not have a particularly raised rim (as November has).
It was a level rim, but there was a fair amount of debris around the rim.
And that was out, about, my 2 o'clock, I guess.
I guess what I was looking for, relative to November, was a bright ejecta blanket; which I don't really see.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: It does look bright on our map here.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Change the subject, we're now should be within line of sight of the LM, so stand by while I get a quick check and see if they can take a comm check.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: (To Scott on the LM circuit) Stand by for a call from Al if you could, guys.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (To Worden) Al, go ahead with it and see if you can raise them.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: (No answer) Hello, Falcon; this is Endeavour.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: (To Scott on the LM circuit) Falcon, would you give Endeavour a call?...
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Falcon and Endeavour, this is Houston.
Evidently neither of you are reading each other.
We'll stand by until you get overhead and give it another try.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I'll keep trying here a couple of times.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Hey, I hear you trying to call.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: ...We're suggesting that you proceed on with the PLSS charge, and we'll be saying goodnight to you shortly.
You're coming up on your sleep period shortly.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And did that Sun compass do you any good, Joe?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Dave, those readings converge pretty well.
They just don't decide between the about 100-meter error we think we have in the two possible landing sites.
But you're very close to being exactly right on.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (No answer) Hello, Falcon; this is Endeavour.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Hey, you're coming in 5 square, David.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Hey, it was super, just super.
And we got the greatest place on the Moon down here.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I think I got you on the last pass, too.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Yeah, that's what they tell me.
Say, can you see Index very well up there?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I could see Index just as clear as a bell.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: I went right down the line: Matthew, Mark, Luke, and Index.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: How do you read now, Dave?
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Okay, let me try another antenna.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Okay; how do you read now?
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: I've got a question for you here...
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: That sure is a pretty sight down there, pal...
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: ...Can you see Index at altitude now?
Could you see any shadows to identify it?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Could you identify Index Crater as you go over the landing site, now?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: (To Dave) And, Falcon, this is Houston.
In a few minutes, while you're stowing the ETB (as per Surface 2-7), we'd like for you to pick up the magazine and
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Yes, I can do that with the naked eye right now.
I'm just coming up on you now, and I can see Index from here.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Stand by (until the conversation with Endeavour is finished).
I guess you're over the hill, because we don't read you now.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I'm just coming up on you.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: As you go by, see if you see any shadows in Index.
I never saw it on the way in.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I've got Index just looking out the side hatch window, right now.
Those four craters ending in Index are just as clear as a bell right now.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Do you see us sort of relative to Index?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I can't see you now, but on the last pass I picked you up, and you're just to the north and a little bit west of Index.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Okay, I think that's about right.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Did you get the coordinates off the map?
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Yes, we got the ones you passed to Houston, and we also got the ones in the Backroom, and I guess we're discussing several kind of numbers now within 100 meters or so of where we really are.
So, I think we're pretty well located.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Well, I'm right over you right now, pal, looking down.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: I hope the view is as fantastic down there as it is up here.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: I'm telling you, it really is!
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Well, we'll do the little things and you do the big things.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: (Pause) Maybe we can get together and compare notes.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Okay, we're about ready to power down for the night, and everything's in good shape down here.
And all we got to do is get a little sleep and get out after it.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: (Pause) I'm keeping your sleeping bag warm for you, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Take care of everything up there.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: I think we may be on split S-band, and you're transmitting to me instead of Gordo.
What can I do for you?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, I didn't change my frequency, Joe.
Say listen, I had a photo pass...(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Alfredo, are you still reading me?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: By the way, the VHF is crystal clear up here.
Soon as you guys get it squared away on the ground, I guess we'll all be tied together.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We'll be standing by for a report of a successful PLSS charge (per Surface 2-7); and (we're) interested in getting you guys in the sack as quickly as possible.
Also, when you load up the ETB, we'll be standing by for frame counts from your cameras.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I have some frame counts for you.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Mag L (also known as AS15-85) is reading 33.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Sounds like we got some beautiful shots already.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Hello, Hadley Base; this is Houston.
Regarding the water separator number 1: Just to give you a warm feeling, it is working perfectly now.
It looks as though, temporarily there, some water run down from the hoses and waterlogged it, but it's working perfectly now.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I'd like to confirm that, on the LMP camera for EVA-1, we use Mag Lima.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Mag Lima on the LMP camera.
And we copied all the frame counts.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Did you copy from me that your water separator number 1 looks good again?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Rog; I thought we called you back on that, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Hadley Base has two charged PLSSs.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, Dave and Jim; this is Houston.
If you two are interested, I could probably arrange for a geology lecture here to put you to sleep.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, Joe, I'm afraid that might keep us awake.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: That was an outstanding job of describing your surroundings, by the way guys.
I'm really...All of us are looking forward to tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I hope we get wound up (for the night) and we can get out there and get close to some of this.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Requesting telemetry switch (to) Low bit rate (in order to conserve power).
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We're not going to ask exactly for a mark when you ingress the
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That sounds like a pretty good game!
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Dave, before you get too well settled there, I forgot to indicate that the Surgeon is requesting a radiation device readout to be included in your crew status report there.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I was afraid you'd get in a position where you couldn't reach those devices.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Oh, no, we weren't thinking of passing the Surgeon.
We just have a number of unscheduled housekeeping chores that we've got to get squared away here, if we are going to settle down for 3 days.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I do want you to get a good night's rest, though.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: It sounds like you'll be able to carry out a very good (physics) experiment with your portable Leaning Tower of Pisa there.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: It really doesn't seem to be leaning that much.
We haven't noticed any...Well, we can see that there's a little tilt in here, but it's no real problem.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: In fact, what, about 10 degrees at the most?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I apologize (in advance) for the question, but are your radiation meters tucked away yet?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Hello, Hadley Base; this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: What can I do for you?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We've got two goodnight questions for you here.
The first, we're trying to unravel some water-pressure data, and we just need to know if you charged the PLSSs using your checklist or using the decal instructions on the PLSSs.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We used the checklist for the water recharge.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger; understand you used the checklist and charged it with water for the specified 5 minutes then.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (Still thinking he's talking to Dave) And, Dave, we've got a major discrepancy in your radiation dosimeter reading.
It's either gone belly-up on us or we miscopied the number which you read.
We'll have to ask you to read it again, please.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: You know that we switched them?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: You know that CDR and CMP's PRD's were interchanged.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: That's affirm, Dave; and the last reading we got from Al was considerably higher than the one we got from you.
The device is either broken or you're being un-irradiated, which seems unreasonable.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, all I can do is look in this very tiny window and look at these very tiny numbers and they say 2501...I guess I can give you a seven on that one (that is, 25017, rather than the 25011 he read earlier).
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And we have no questions from Houston.
We'll say a pleasant good night to the two of you and look forward to tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I guess we don't have any alarm clock, so if somebody'll give us the word, we'll be standing by.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I wouldn't be at all surprised.
You're liable to get the word from down here.
(Pause) And it's been an outstanding day.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We'll see you in the morning.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I just let you know that we're all tucked into the
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Good night and don't fall out.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Pause) There's no place to go if I did!
transcript.speaker: Scott
